metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Choral Music 10 develops students' vocal technique, music literacy, and ensemble singing skills. Students learn to produce a healthy, expressive choral tone, develop sight-singing abilities, and rehearse and perform choral repertoire from diverse musical traditions. Critical listening, personal reflection, and collaborative rehearsal are central. First Peoples vocal traditions are explored with appropriate cultural respect. This course opens pathways to Choral Music 11 and 12.
metadata.general_description: A senior choral music course developing vocal technique, choral ensemble skills, music literacy, and musical understanding — with an emphasis on healthy singing, expressive performance, and diverse choral repertoire.

big_ideas.statement: Music provides opportunities to gain insight into the perspectives and experiences of people from a variety of times, places, and cultures.
big_ideas.elaboration: Choral music from diverse traditions — from Renaissance motets to contemporary Indigenous song — opens windows into different cultural values, aesthetic sensibilities, and human experiences.

big_ideas.statement: The voice is an instrument for artistic and communal expression.
big_ideas.elaboration: Developing the singing voice requires physical awareness, technical discipline, and artistic sensitivity. The choral ensemble transforms individual voices into a collective instrument of extraordinary expressive power.

big_ideas.statement: Creative growth requires patience, readiness to take risks, and willingness to try new approaches.
big_ideas.elaboration: Vocal development is a sustained process of patient practice, experimentation, and refinement. Growth as a singer comes through deliberate effort, receptivity to feedback, and willingness to explore new vocal and musical territory.

big_ideas.statement: Individual and collective expression through music is rooted in history, culture, and community.
big_ideas.elaboration: Choral singing is one of humanity's oldest communal practices, carrying cultural memory, spiritual meaning, and collective identity across generations and continents.

competencies.raw: Develop vocal technique and musicianship; apply elements of music expressively; use imagination and inquiry to explore musical ideas; create through improvisation and composition activities.
i_can_statements: I can develop healthy vocal technique including breath support, resonance, and accurate intonation.
I can apply musical elements — dynamics, phrasing, diction, blend — expressively in choral performance.
I can explore musical ideas through improvisation, call-and-response, and short composition activities.

competencies.raw: Analyze and respond to choral music using music-specific vocabulary; apply feedback to improve vocal performance; examine music in social, cultural, and historical context.
i_can_statements: I can analyze choral music using accurate terminology: voice parts, blend, intonation, form, and style.
I can apply feedback from conductors and peers to improve my vocal performance and ensemble contribution.
I can examine the social, cultural, and historical contexts that shape the choral repertoire I perform.

competencies.raw: Perform prepared and sight-read music for audiences; document musical learning and growth; communicate musical understanding.
i_can_statements: I can perform prepared choral repertoire for an audience with technical skill and expressive commitment.
I can sight-sing at a grade-appropriate level using solfège or other sight-singing systems.
I can document my choral learning through practice reflection, listening journals, and recordings.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate responsibility in rehearsal and performance; respect self, ensemble, and audience; explore diverse vocal traditions including First Peoples; build community connections through music.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate responsibility and professionalism in choral rehearsal and performance.
I can engage respectfully with diverse choral and vocal traditions, including First Peoples song.
I can build community connections through shared choral performance experiences.

areas.name: Vocal Technique
areas.raw: Vocal production: breath support, resonance, intonation, diction.
topics: Breath management: diaphragmatic breathing, breath support, and phrasing
Resonance and tone: placement, vowel formation, and register blending
Intonation: equal temperament, harmonic tuning, and listening in ensemble
Diction: consonant clarity and vowel unification across languages
Vocal health: care and warm-up practices

areas.name: Music Literacy and Sight-Singing
topics: Sight-singing systems: solfège (movable do), numbers, or other approaches
Notation: reading treble and bass clef parts at appropriate levels
Rhythm: counting complex rhythms, syncopation, and mixed meter
Score reading: following conductor markings, rehearsal numbers, and musical directions

areas.name: Ensemble Performance and Repertoire
areas.raw: Choral ensemble skills and diverse repertoire.
topics: Voice parts: soprano, alto, tenor, bass (SATB) and variable voice voicings
Balance and blend: listening within the ensemble, matching vowels and tone
Rehearsal strategies: section rehearsals, problem-solving passages, efficient rehearsal habits
Diverse choral repertoire: Renaissance, Baroque, Romantic, contemporary, world, and Indigenous

areas.name: Music History and Cultural Contexts
areas.raw: Choral traditions from diverse cultures; First Peoples vocal music; music history.
topics: Choral history: major periods from Gregorian chant to contemporary choral writing
First Peoples vocal traditions: songlines, ceremonial music, drum songs, and protocols
Global choral traditions: Gospel, African choral, Eastern European, and others
Community choral singing: barbershop, church choirs, and culturally specific traditions
